1: מִקֵּץ שְׁנָתַיִם יָמִים (miqqēṣ šĕnātayim yāmîm) — "at the end of two full years." Miqqēṣ (at the end of — from qēṣ, end, limit; cf. 8:6 where the same phrase marks the end of Noah's waiting period). Šĕnātayim (dual: two years) + yāmîm (days — a supplementary indicator of full years; "two years of days" = two complete years; the idiom emphasizes that the entire period elapsed). Two full years after the cupbearer's restoration and Joseph's forgotten petition — the divine timetable operates on its own schedule. עֹמֵד עַל-הַיְאֹר (standing by the Nile) — yĕʾôr (the Nile — an Egyptian loanword, from Egyptian j'rw, "river/canal"; the standard Hebrew word for the Nile throughout the Exodus narrative; its presence here is one of several authentically Egyptian elements in the Joseph narrative).
2: שֶׁבַע פָּרוֹת בְּרִיאוֹת וּטֹבוֹת מַרְאֶה (šeḇaʿ pārôt bĕrîʾôt ûṭōḇôt marʾeh) — "seven cows, fat and good of appearance." Pārāh (cow — female bovine specifically; appears 26× in this chapter, uniquely concentrated; in Egyptian religion, the cow was sacred to Hathor, goddess of fertility and beauty; the dream operates within the Egyptian religious register without acknowledging it). Bārîʾ (fat, healthy, well-fed — from a root meaning to be fat/strong; the same adjective used in Dan 1:15 for the healthier-looking Daniel and his companions). בָּאַחוּ (bāʾaḥû, among the reeds) — ʾāḥû (marsh grass, reeds — an Egyptian loanword from Egyptian ꜣḫw, "marsh plants"; a rare word in Hebrew appearing only here and in Gen 41:18 and Job 8:11; its Egyptian origin anchors the dream in the Nile-delta landscape).
3: רָעוֹת מַרְאֶה וְדַקּוֹת בָּשָׂר (rāʿôt marʾeh wĕdaqqôt bāśār) — "ugly of appearance and lean of flesh." Rāʿ marʾeh (ugly/bad of appearance) — the opposite of the good-appearance formula; daq bāśār (thin of flesh) — the emaciated cattle of famine; bāśār (flesh, body) used for the physical condition of the animal. The seven lean cows standing beside the seven fat ones on the riverbank create the dream's symbolic tableau: the abundance and the famine exist simultaneously in the vision before the historical sequence unfolds.
4: וַתֹּאכַלְנָה הַפָּרוֹת רָעוֹת הַמַּרְאֶה — "the ugly-looking cows ate the seven fat, good cows." The narrative reversal embedded in the dream: the weaker consume the stronger. Wayyîqaṣ parʿōh (Pharaoh woke up) — the first awakening between the two dreams; the structure of double-dream-with-awakening is a recognized dream-type in ANE literature; the doubling confirms and intensifies the message (cf. v. 32). Pharaoh's waking — seeing it was a dream — increases his disturbance (v. 8) rather than relieving it.
5: שֶׁבַע שִׁבֳּלִים עֹלוֹת בְּקָנֶה אֶחָד (šeḇaʿ šibbŏlîm ʿōlôt bĕqāneh ʾeḥāḏ) — "seven heads of grain growing on a single stalk." Šibbōlet (head of grain, ear of grain — the same word that becomes the famous pronunciation test in Judg 12:6 ["say šibbōlet"]; here its agricultural meaning governs). Bĕrîʾôt wĕṭōḇôt (healthy and good) — the same pair of adjectives as the fat cows (v. 2); the parallel vocabulary between the two dreams signals their identical meaning. A single stalk producing seven heads is itself a symbol of extraordinary abundance.
6: שֶׁבַע שִׁבֳּלִים דַּקּוֹת וּשְׁדוּפֹת קָדִים (šeḇaʿ šibbŏlîm daqqôt ûšĕḏûpōt qāḏîm) — "seven thin heads scorched by the east wind." Šĕḏûpāh (scorched, blasted — Qal passive participle of šāḏap, to scorch, blight; the word for crop-disease/blight appears in Deut 28:22 as a covenant curse; 1 Kgs 8:37; Amos 4:9; Hag 2:17 — grain-blight is always in the context of divine judgment). Qāḏîm (east wind — the hot, dry sirocco from the east/southeast; cf. Ezek 17:10; 19:12; Hos 13:15; Jon 4:8; the east wind withers, scatters, and destroys in OT poetry and prophecy).
7: וַתִּבְלַעְנָה הַשִּׁבֳּלִים הַדַּקּוֹת — "the thin heads swallowed the seven fat heads." The same swallowing-verb (bālaʿ) as the lean cows in v. 4 (tōʾkalnāh); the second dream uses a different verb but the same action-structure. Wayyîqaṣ parʿōh wĕhinnēh ḥălôm (Pharaoh woke up — and behold it was a dream) — the wĕhinnēh (and behold) of abrupt realization; the twice-awakened Pharaoh now knows he has had two vivid dreams; his morning disturbance (v. 8) shows they did not feel like ordinary dreams.
8: וַיְהִי בַבֹּקֶר וַתִּפָּעֶם רוּחוֹ (wayhî ḇabbōqer wattippāʿem rûḥô) — "in the morning his spirit was troubled." Pāʿam (Niphal: to be disturbed, agitated — used for the agitation of the spirit before divine revelation; cf. Dan 2:1, 3 where Nebuchadnezzar's spirit is nippāʿam after his dream; the Niphal expresses an involuntary disturbance of the interior). חַרְטֻמֵּי מִצְרָיִם (ḥarṭummê miṣrāyim, the magicians of Egypt) — ḥarṭummîm (an Egyptian loanword from Egyptian ḥr-tp, "chief lector priest" or "keeper of the sacred books"; the professional dream-interpreters and ritual specialists of the Egyptian court; the same word appears in Exod 7:11, 22; 8:3, 14-15; 9:11 where the same class fails against Moses' signs). Their failure here is theologically significant: the interpretive tradition of Egypt cannot read what God has revealed; וְחַכְמֵי מִצְרָיִם (the wise men of Egypt) — the ḥăḵāmîm (wisdom-professionals) whose failure underscores that ḥōkmāh (wisdom) in Egypt is insufficient to decode God's revelation.
9: אֶת-חֲטָאַי אֲנִי מַזְכִּיר הַיּוֹם (ʾet-ḥăṭāʾay ʾănî mazkîr hayyôm) — "my shortcomings I am making known today." The cupbearer uses ḥēṭ (sin, offense, shortcoming — the standard word for sin: moral wrong, offense against a superior, falling short of a standard); here likely referring to a failure of his official duty or his neglect of Joseph. Mazkîr (Hiphil participle of zākar, to remember; Hiphil = to cause to remember, to mention, to record) — "I am causing to be remembered" = I am now bringing to mind what I should have mentioned before; the verb of memory is used for both the cupbearer's self-disclosure and for Joseph's earlier "remembering" of the dreams (42:9).
10: The cupbearer recaps the events of ch. 40: Pharaoh's anger (qāṣap, to be angry — the same verb used for Pharaoh's anger in 40:2), the imprisonment in the captain of the guard's house (bêt śar haṭṭabbāḥîm — the same house where Joseph was confined). The recap serves to introduce Joseph to Pharaoh's attention as a reliable dream-interpreter with a proven track record.
11: וַנַּחַלְמָה חֲלוֹם בְּלַיְלָה אֶחָד אֲנִי וָהוּא — "we each dreamed a dream in one night, he and I." The single-night double-dream confirms the simultaneous divine communication to both prisoners. Kĕpitron ḥălōmô (according to the interpretation of his dream) — pitrôn (interpretation — an Egyptian loanword from Egyptian ptr, "to understand/interpret"; appears only in the Joseph narrative: 40:5, 8, 12, 16, 18; 41:11); the concentration of this loanword in the Joseph narrative is part of its Egyptian vocabulary cluster.
12: נַעַר עִבְרִי (naʿar ʿiḇrî, a young Hebrew man) — "a young Hebrew there with us." Naʿar (young man — the same word applied to Joseph in 37:2; the cupbearer remembers Joseph as a subordinate; the word carries the social implication of servant/junior). ʿIḇrî (Hebrew — the ethnic designation; from ʿēḇer, one who crosses over; the term is used to designate Israelites to non-Israelites throughout the Exodus narrative: 1:15, 16, 19; 2:7, 11, 13; 3:18 etc.; the cupbearer identifies Joseph by his ethnicity, not his name or status). ʿEḇeḏ śar haṭṭabbāḥîm (servant of the captain of the guard) — the social position the cupbearer remembers Joseph as holding; Joseph was Potiphar's servant, then the captain's prisoner — the cupbearer's memory focuses on the prison context.
13: כַּאֲשֶׁר פָּתַר-לָנוּ כֵּן הָיָה (kaʾăšer pātar-lānû kēn hāyāh) — "just as he interpreted to us, so it was." Pātar (to interpret a dream — a verb of uncertain etymology, possibly also Egyptian in background; used exclusively with dream-interpretation in Genesis: 40:8, 12, 16, 18, 22; 41:8, 12, 13, 15); the fulfilled interpretation is the certification of Joseph's interpretive gift: it was not guesswork but genuine revelation. Wayyitlēhû (and he was impaled) — tālāh (to hang, impale — the form of execution; whether this was hanging on a gallows or impalement on a post is debated; the verb covers both; the baker's fate is confirmed as the dream predicted).
14: וַיְרִיצֻהוּ מִן-הַבּוֹר (wayyĕrîṣûhû min-habbôr) — "they rushed him from the pit/dungeon." Rîṣ (Hiphil: to cause to run, to bring quickly — the urgency of the royal summons); bôr (pit, cistern, dungeon — the same word as the dry cistern of 37:24 into which the brothers threw Joseph; Joseph's prison in Egypt is also called a bôr; the word brackets Joseph's entire period of humiliation: from the pit in Canaan to the pit-dungeon in Egypt, and now the release from both). וַיְגַלַּח וַיְחַלֵּף שִׂמְלֹתָיו (he shaved and changed his clothes) — gāllaḥ (to shave — Egyptians, unlike Hebrews, shaved their entire bodies including facial hair; Herodotus 2.36 notes that Egyptian priests shave; Joseph's shaving is cultural adaptation for the royal court appearance; Pharaoh's court required a clean-shaven face).
15: Pharaoh's statement — "I have heard it said of you that when you hear a dream you can interpret it" — the reputation precedes Joseph; the cupbearer's report has established him as a dream-interpreter. The verb šāmaʿ (to hear, to hear about) — Pharaoh has "heard" about Joseph's gift; the same hearing-verb that drives the patriarchal narrative of providence.
16: בִּלְעָדָי אֱלֹהִים יַעֲנֶה אֶת-שְׁלוֹם פַּרְעֹה (bilʿāḏāy ʾĔlōhîm yaʿăneh ʾet-šĕlôm parʿōh) — "apart from me — God will answer Pharaoh's welfare." Bilʿāḏāy (apart from me, without me — the disclaimer formula; Joseph explicitly removes himself from the interpretive credit before he has even heard the dream). Yaʿăneh (Qal imperfect of ʿānāh, to answer, respond — future action) — the answer will come; its source is God, not Joseph. Šĕlôm parʿōh (Pharaoh's šālôm) — not a greeting but "what will bring Pharaoh to peace/resolution"; the šālôm-word applied to the resolution of Pharaoh's disturbed spirit (v. 8). Joseph's humility before Pharaoh echoes and inverts the brothers' self-humiliation before Joseph — one is genuine, the other forced.
17: Pharaoh retells his dreams in vv. 17-24 — a near-verbatim repetition of vv. 1-7, with minor variations that the narrator includes: Pharaoh says "I had never seen such ugly cows in all the land of Egypt" (v. 19 — an intensification not in the original account); "they looked just as ugly as before" (v. 21 — a detail added in the retelling). The variations in Pharaoh's retelling versus the narrator's original account is a common feature of biblical narrative (cf. Abraham's servant retelling the commission story in ch. 24 with variations); the repetition with minor variations is not an error but reflects the narrator's technique of characterization through speech.
18: The fat cows in Pharaoh's retelling: bĕrîʾôt ûṭōḇôt marʾeh (fat and good of appearance) — the same adjectives as v. 2 but in reverse order (v. 2: sleek and fat; v. 18: fat and sleek); the minor reordering is characteristic of retelling. The detail of the Nile-bank setting is preserved: the connection between the Nile, Egypt's fertility, and Pharaoh's dream is maintained throughout the retelling.
19: וְהִנֵּה שֶׁבַע-פָּרוֹת אֲחֵרוֹת עֹלוֹת אַחֲרֵיהֶן דַּלּוֹת וְרָעוֹת תֹּאַר מְאֹד — Pharaoh adds the intensifier mĕʾōḏ (very much) in his retelling: "very ugly" — the emotional impact of the disturbing cows is expressed more emphatically in his direct report. לֹא-רָאִיתִי כָהֵנָּה בְּכָל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם לָרֹעַ (I had never seen such ugly cows in all the land of Egypt) — the superlative "never seen such" is Pharaoh's subjective memory-addition to the dream account; Egypt is famous for its productive cattle; cows this poor were an aberration from the Egyptian agricultural norm.
20: The eating of the fat cows by the lean: Pharaoh's retelling compresses the narrative slightly; the detail that "no one could tell they had eaten them" appears in v. 21 as an addition unique to Pharaoh's version. The famine that is coming will be so severe that the years of abundance will be completely forgotten — the retrospective look will show no evidence of prior prosperity.
21: וְלֹא נוֹדַע כִּי-בָאוּ אֶל-קִרְבֶּנָה (wĕlōʾ nôḏaʿ kî-ḇāʾû ʾel-qirḇennāh) — "no one could tell that they had entered their belly." Nôḏaʿ (Niphal of yāḏaʿ, to be known — passive: it was not known); the visible condition of the lean cows after consuming the fat ones was unchanged — the sign of an absolute famine that leaves no trace of former abundance. Qereb (inner part, belly — used for the interior of the body; the cows' physical appearance contradicted what they had consumed).
22: The grain dream retold by Pharaoh: the single-stalk-seven-heads image is repeated. The parallel structure of the two dream-pairs (cows/grain; fat-then-lean/full-then-withered) is noted by Pharaoh himself in his retelling — he connects both dreams into a single interpretive puzzle. The accuracy of the retelling confirms that the dream was vivid and memorable.
23: צְנֻמוֹת דַּקּוֹת שְׁדוּפוֹת קָדִים (ṣĕnumôt daqqôt šĕḏûpôt qāḏîm) — "withered, thin, scorched by the east wind." Pharaoh adds a third adjective in his retelling: ṣānûm (withered, dried up — from a root related to dryness; appearing only here) alongside daqqôt (thin) and šĕḏûpôt qāḏîm (east-wind scorched). The intensification of the description of the withered grain parallels the intensification of the ugly-cow description (v. 19).
24: וָאֹמַר אֶל-הַחַרְטֻמִּים וְאֵין מַגִּיד לִי (wāʾōmar ʾel-haḥarṭummîm wĕʾên maggîḏ lî) — "I told this to the magicians, but none could explain it to me." The ḥarṭummîm's failure is confirmed in Pharaoh's own words: professional Egyptian dream-interpretation could not decode what God revealed. Maggîḏ (Hiphil participle of nāḡaḏ, to tell, declare, make known — the same root used for Joseph's self-disclosure: wayyaggēḏ yôsēp when he reveals himself in 45:1); no one among Egypt's professionals could "make known" the interpretation.
25: חֲלוֹם פַּרְעֹה אֶחָד הוּא (ḥălôm parʿōh ʾeḥāḏ hûʾ) — "the dream of Pharaoh is one." Joseph's opening declaration: the two dreams have a single meaning. ʾEḥāḏ (one) — the unifying declaration; the God of Israel who is ʾeḥāḏ (one, Deut 6:4) speaks in one message even through two dreams. אֵת אֲשֶׁר הָאֱלֹהִים עֹשֶׂה הִגִּיד לְפַרְעֹה (what God is doing, he has declared to Pharaoh) — higgîḏ (Hiphil of nāgaḏ, to declare, to make known — the prophetic vocabulary of divine disclosure); the dreams are not random or self-generated but divine disclosure. Note the shift: the ḥarṭummîm could not "make known" the interpretation; God "makes known" what he is about to do.
26: שֶׁבַע פָּרֹת הַטֹּבֹת שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים הֵנָּה — "the seven good cows are seven years." Joseph gives the interpretation in the form of equation: dream-symbol = historical reality. The identification of symbolic elements with years is standard ANE dream-interpretation; Joseph's identification connects Egypt's agricultural patterns (Nile flooding → crop production) to a seven-year divine plan. Hûʾ ḥălôm ʾeḥāḏ hûʾ (it is one dream, it is one) — the emphatic repetition of the unity-declaration; the doubling of ʾeḥāḏ hûʾ in v. 26 reinforces what v. 25 established.
27: שֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי רָעָב הֵנָּה (šeḇaʿ šĕnê rāʿāḇ hēnnāh) — "these are seven years of famine." Rāʿāḇ (famine, hunger — the word used for the famines of Abraham's era [12:10], Isaac's era [26:1], and now the great famine of Joseph's time; it will recur in the era of Ruth and Naomi [Ruth 1:1] and the time of Elijah [1 Kgs 18:2]). The parallel identification: seven good cows = seven good years; seven lean cows = seven famine years; seven good heads = seven good years; seven withered heads = seven famine years. The double-dream means the meaning is confirmed twice: Joseph will explain the mechanism in v. 32.
28: The restatement of Joseph's summary: "it is just as I said to Pharaoh: God has shown Pharaoh what he is about to do." Hĕrʾāh (Hiphil of rāʾāh, to cause to see, to show — God caused Pharaoh to see/know his plan). The repetition (v. 25: "God has declared"; v. 28: "God has shown") uses two different disclosure verbs (nāḡaḏ and rāʾāh), both attributing the dreams' content to divine initiative.
29: שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים בָּאוֹת שָׂבָע גָּדוֹל (šeḇaʿ šānîm bāʾôt śāḇāʿ gāḏôl) — "seven years of great abundance are coming." Śāḇāʿ (abundance, satiety — from śāḇaʿ, to be satisfied, to have enough; used for the complete satisfaction of having eaten well; Prov 3:10; Joel 2:26; the word describes not just sufficiency but fullness beyond need). Bāʾôt (Qal active participle, plural: coming — the participle of approach; the abundance is already on its way, already in motion).
30: וְנִשְׁכַּח כָּל-הַשָּׂבָע (wĕniškaḥ kol-haśśāḇāʿ) — "all the abundance will be forgotten." Šāḵaḥ (to forget — the verb that the cupbearer used when he "forgot" Joseph [40:23]; now the entire period of Egyptian abundance will be forgotten by the famine that follows; the forgetting-verb is ironically rich in the Joseph narrative). Wĕḵillāh hārāʿāḇ ʾet-hāʾāreṣ (the famine will ravage/destroy the land) — kālāh (Piel: to finish off, consume, destroy — the intensive destruction; the Piel is stronger than the simple completion: it destroys thoroughly).
31: וְלֹא-יִוָּדַע הַשָּׂבָע בָּאָרֶץ (wĕlōʾ-yiwwāḏaʿ haśśāḇāʿ bāʾāreṣ) — "the abundance will not be known in the land." The future passive: the abundance will not be knowable; it will leave no evidence. This echoes the dream's detail (v. 21): the lean cows that consumed the fat ones "looked just as ugly as before" — the famine erases the abundance visually in the dream and historically in reality.
32: וְעַל הִשָּׁנוֹת הַחֲלוֹם אֶל-פַּרְעֹה פַּעֲמָיִם כִּי-נָכוֹן הַדָּבָר מֵעִם הָאֱלֹהִים — "the reason the dream was given twice is that the matter is established from with God." Nāḵôn (Niphal of kûn, to be established, confirmed, prepared — the certainty of fixity; a Niphal participle: "it is established"); mēʿim hāʾĔlōhîm (from with God — ʿim = with, beside; the matter issues from God's deliberated will). וּמְמַהֵר הָאֱלֹהִים לַעֲשֹׂתוֹ (and God is hastening to do it) — māhar (Piel: to hasten, act urgently — the same urgency as the Passover eating [Exod 12:11: "you shall eat it with urgency"]; the divine plan is not only certain but imminent). The two-dream confirmation (v. 32) is a theological principle: God confirms his word through repetition (cf. Deut 17:6; 19:15 — two witnesses; 2 Cor 13:1).
33: וְעַתָּה יֵרֶא פַרְעֹה אִישׁ נָבוֹן וְחָכָם (wĕʿattāh yēreh parʿōh ʾîš nāḇôn wĕḥāḵām) — "now let Pharaoh look for a discerning and wise man." Joseph moves from interpretation to policy recommendation without waiting to be asked — an audacious move for a prisoner. Nāḇôn (discerning, insightful — Niphal participle of bîn, to understand, perceive; the naḇôn is the one who has penetrating perception; cf. Prov 1:5; 10:13; the combination nāḇôn wĕḥāḵām appears in Deut 1:13 for the qualifications of tribal leaders and in 1 Sam 16:18 for David's qualities). The qualities Joseph recommends are exactly the ones Pharaoh will identify in Joseph himself (v. 39).
34: וִיפְקֵד פְּקִדֹת עַל-הָאָרֶץ (wîpaqēḏ pĕqiḏōt ʿal-hāʾāreṣ) — "let him appoint overseers over the land." The cognate accusative pāqaḏ pĕqiḏōt (appoint appointments/officials) — the pāqaḏ verb is the covenantal-visit word: YHWH "visits" his people in judgment and redemption (21:1; 50:24-25); here used for the administrative appointment of commissioners; the same root as pāqîd (official, overseer; cf. Esth 2:3). ʿAmmēš ʾet-ʾereṣ miṣrāyim (to take a fifth of the harvest) — the twenty-percent grain tax during the seven years of abundance; the taxation plan concentrates the surplus under Pharaoh's authority.
35: וְיִצְבְּרוּ-בָר תַּחַת יַד-פַּרְעֹה (wĕyiṣbĕrû-ḇār taḥat yaḏ-parʿōh) — "let them collect grain under Pharaoh's authority." Ṣāḇar (to heap up, accumulate — used in Exod 8:10 for the heaped frogs; Zech 9:3 for heaped silver; here for the systematic accumulation of grain surpluses). Bār (processed/clean grain — specifically grain that has been cleaned and separated from chaff; the higher-quality designation emphasizes that the stored food will be usable). Taḥat yaḏ-parʿōh (under the hand of Pharaoh) — under royal authority; the centralized storage system under Pharaoh's control is the key of the plan.
36: לְמִחְיָה לָאָרֶץ (lĕmiḥyāh lāʾāreṣ) — "for the sustaining of life for the land." Miḥyāh (sustenance, means of life — from ḥāyāh, to live; literally "that which gives life"; appears in Judg 6:4 for the grain left to the Israelites by Midian; the word for life-sustaining food). The goal of the storage plan is explicitly stated: wĕlōʾ-tiḵkaret hāʾāreṣ bārāʿāḇ (so that the land may not be cut off by the famine) — kārat (to be cut off — the covenant-curse word; to be "cut off" is the penalty for covenant violation [Exod 12:15, 19; 31:14; Lev 7:20-21]; here it is famine's threat to the land).
37: וַיִּיטַב הַדָּבָר בְּעֵינֵי פַרְעֹה (wayyîṭaḇ haddāḇār bĕʿênê parʿōh) — "the plan seemed good in Pharaoh's eyes." The standard formula of approval: ṭōḇ bĕʿênê (good in the eyes of) = to please, to seem right to; the idiom of subjective acceptance. Ûḇĕʿênê kol-ʿăḇāḏāyw (and in the eyes of all his servants) — unanimous approval; the plan was not just acceptable to Pharaoh but to his entire court. The universal approval legitimizes Joseph's appointment: it is not Pharaoh's whim but court consensus.
38: הֲנִמְצָא כָזֶה אִישׁ אֲשֶׁר רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים בּוֹ (hăniṣmāʾ ḵāzeh ʾîš ʾăšer rûaḥ ʾĔlōhîm bô) — "can we find a man like this, one in whom is the spirit of God?" Rûaḥ ʾĔlōhîm (spirit of God) — on the lips of Pharaoh, this phrase likely means "divine spirit" in the Egyptian sense (the spirit of the gods); in the Hebrew narrative it is presented as the Spirit of Israel's God. The same phrase as Gen 1:2 where the Spirit of God hovered over the waters at creation; and as Exod 31:3; 35:31 where Bezalel is filled with the Spirit of God for the tabernacle's artistry. Pharaoh inadvertently identifies the source of Joseph's gifts: the Spirit that creates and orders is the same Spirit that reveals and interprets.
39: אַחֲרֵי הוֹדִיעַ אֱלֹהִים אוֹתְךָ אֶת-כָּל-זֹאת (ʾaḥărê hôḏîaʿ ʾĔlōhîm ʾôtĕḵā ʾet-kol-zōʾt) — "since God has made all this known to you." Hôḏîaʿ (Hiphil of yāḏaʿ, to cause to know, to reveal — the Hiphil causative; God caused Joseph to know; the knowledge did not originate with Joseph). Nāḇôn wĕḥāḵām (discerning and wise) — the exact qualities Joseph had recommended in v. 33 are now applied to Joseph himself by Pharaoh: the recommender is identified as the best candidate. The narrative's wit: Joseph did not nominate himself but described himself.
40: עַל-בֵּיתִי יִשַּׁק כָּל-עַמִּי (ʿal-bêtî yiššaq kol-ʿammî) — "all my people shall be subject to your orders." Nāšaq (Qal: to kiss; but here in the context of the formula, likely a homophone meaning "to submit, to be ruled by"; some read this as "all my people will be at your beck and call" — the ruler-submission idiom); the idiom is debated but the sense is clear: Joseph has supreme authority over the Egyptian populace. Raq hakkissēʾ ʾeḡdal mimmeka (only the throne will I be greater than you) — the sole exception to Joseph's authority is Pharaoh himself; the throne is the only thing Joseph does not receive.
41: רְאֵה נָתַתִּי אֹתְךָ עַל כָּל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם (rĕʾēh nāṯattî ʾōtĕḵā ʿal kol-ʾereṣ miṣrāyim) — "see, I have set you over all the land of Egypt." The perfect tense nāṯattî (I have given/set — a performative perfect: the declaration makes it so; the speech-act is the appointment); Pharaoh's word of installation is itself the legal act. Rĕʾēh (see! behold! — the imperative used for drawing attention to a decisive action or proclamation; cf. 41:41; Deut 1:8: "See, I have given you the land").
42: טַבַּעְתּוֹ מֵעַל יָדוֹ (ṭabbaʿtô mēʿal yāḏô) — "his signet ring from his hand." Ṭabbaʿat (signet ring — the seal-ring used for authenticating royal documents in wax; the transfer of the signet ring is the transfer of Pharaoh's authority for official acts; cf. Esth 3:10; 8:2 where the same ring-transfer signals authority). בִּגְדֵי-שֵׁשׁ (bigdê-šēš, garments of fine linen) — šēš (fine linen — an Egyptian word for high-quality bleached linen; the same word used for the tabernacle's linen hangings: Exod 26:1, 31, 36; 27:9, 16, 18; and for the priestly garments: Exod 28:5-6, 8, 15, 39). רְבִד הַזָּהָב (rĕḇîḏ hazzāhāḇ, the gold chain/necklace) — rĕḇîḏ (necklace, chain — hapax apart from Ezek 16:11; the gold neck-chain is an Egyptian royal honor; the three tokens of appointment: signet ring + fine linen + gold chain = the full Egyptian investiture ceremony).
43: אַבְרֵךְ (ʾaḇrēḵ) — the word called out before Joseph's chariot; a genuine hapax legomenon of uncertain etymology. Proposed readings: (1) Egyptian ib-r-k or similar = "bow the knee/head in homage"; (2) Hebrew root bāraḵ (to bless/kneel) with the Egyptian definite article or prefix; (3) Akkadian cognate; the LXX transliterates it without translation (abrek), suggesting it was already opaque to early translators. The form and function are clear — a cry of honor and submission as the second-chariot passes — even if the precise linguistic origin is disputed. Joseph rides in Pharaoh's second chariot (merkĕḇet hamišneh): the chariot of the second-in-command, the vice-royal chariot.
44: אֲנִי פַרְעֹה וּבִלְעָדֶיךָ לֹא-יָרִים אִישׁ אֶת-יָדוֹ וְאֶת-רַגְלוֹ — "I am Pharaoh, but without you no man will lift hand or foot in all Egypt." The formula "I am Pharaoh" (ʾănî parʿōh) echoes the covenant-declaration formula "I am YHWH" (ʾănî YHWH); Pharaoh uses the royal self-identification to validate the decree. Wĕḇilʿāḏeḵā (but without you — the same idiom Joseph used in v. 16: "apart from me, God will answer"; now Pharaoh uses it: "apart from you, no one acts"). The irony is complete: Joseph who said "apart from me" now has Pharaoh say "apart from you."
45: צָפְנַת פַּעְנֵחַ (ṣāpĕnat paʿnēaḥ, Zaphenath-Paneah) — an Egyptian throne-name given to Joseph; its exact etymology is disputed: proposed readings include Egyptian ḏd-p-ntr-iw.f-ʿnḫ ("the god speaks and he lives") or ṣnṯ-pꜥ-nḥ ("sustainer of life"); the form is authentically Egyptian despite uncertainty about the precise original. אָסְנַת בַּת-פּוֹטִי פֶרַע (Asenath daughter of Potiphera) — ʾĀsĕnat from Egyptian ns-nt ("she who belongs to Neith," the Egyptian goddess of wisdom and war); Pôṭî peraʿ (Potiphera — "gift of Re/the sun god"; cf. the priest Potiphar, possibly the same name-form; both are Egyptian theophoric names containing the sun-god Ra/Re). Joseph's wife is the daughter of the priest of ʾÔn (On = Heliopolis, the center of Egyptian sun-worship, the city of the great temple of Re); Joseph is integrated into Egypt's highest priestly aristocracy through marriage.
46: וְיוֹסֵף בֶּן-שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה בְּעָמְדוֹ לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה (wĕyôsēp ben-šĕlōšîm šānāh bĕʿomĕdô lipnê parʿōh) — "Joseph was thirty years old when he stood before Pharaoh." The age datum: thirty is significant in the OT as the age of priestly service (Num 4:3, 23, 30, 35, 39, 43, 47 — Levites begin at thirty), of David's kingship (2 Sam 5:4), and of John the Baptist's and Jesus' public ministry (Luke 3:23: "about thirty years old"). Joseph was seventeen when sold (37:2) — thirteen years of servitude and imprisonment before his exaltation. The narrator supplies the chronological marker to anchor the subsequent events.
47: וַתַּעַשׂ הָאָרֶץ בְּשֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי הַשָּׂבָע לִקְמָצִים (wattaʿaś hāʾāreṣ bĕšeḇaʿ šĕnê haśśāḇāʿ liqmāṣîm) — "the land produced by handfuls in the seven years of abundance." Qāmāṣ (handful — the standard grain-measure of a handful or fistful; here used to express superabundance: the land produced by the handfuls; the idiom suggests that the grain was so plentiful it could be gathered by the handful and it was still abundant).
48: Joseph's collection system: wayyiṣbōr kol-ʾōkel šeḇaʿ šānîm (he stored all the food of the seven years) — the systematic collection; food from the fields surrounding each city is stored in that city (bĕtôḵāh, within it). The decentralized storage in multiple cities prevents a single point of failure and reduces transportation costs during distribution. The administrative efficiency of Joseph's plan reflects the sophisticated Egyptian granary system (attested in archaeological records of Egyptian grain storage).
49: וַיִּצְבֹּר יוֹסֵף בָּר כְּחוֹל הַיָּם (wayyiṣbōr yôsēp bār kĕḥôl hayyām) — "Joseph stored up grain like the sand of the sea." The sand-of-the-sea formula — the same comparison used for the promise to Abraham (22:17: "as numerous as the sand on the seashore"); now the covenant-promise of innumerable descendants finds an ironic parallel in the innumerable grain Joseph stores. ʿAḏ kî-ḥāḏal lisepōr kî-ʾên mispar (until he stopped counting because it was beyond number) — the bookkeeping stopped because the quantity exceeded recording capacity; the storage was literally uncountable.
50: וּלְיוֹסֵף יֻלַּד שְׁנֵי בָנִים (ûlĕyôsēp yullaḏ šĕnê ḇānîm) — "two sons were born to Joseph." The Niphal passive: were born to him; the children come before the famine. Liphê bôʾ šĕnat hārāʿāḇ (before the year of famine came) — the chronological marker places the birth of Joseph's sons in the abundance period; the children are born in Egypt, to an Egyptian wife, in the years of plenty — fully embedded in Egyptian life. The names Joseph gives them will explain his theological interpretation of his exile.
51: מְנַשֶּׁה כִּי-נַשַּׁנִי אֱלֹהִים אֶת-כָּל-עֲמָלִי (mĕnaššeh kî-naššanî ʾĔlōhîm ʾet-kol-ʿămālî) — "Manasseh, for God has made me forget all my trouble." The name-etymology is explicit: mĕnaššeh from naššanî (made me forget — Piel of nāšāh, to forget; the Piel causative: God caused the forgetting). ʿĀmāl (trouble, toil — the painful labor; cf. Eccl 2:11 where ʿāmāl is all the toilsome striving under the sun; Job 3:10 where ʿāmāl is life's suffering) + bêt ʾāḇî (my father's household) — Joseph attributes his forgetting of the pain of his father's house to God's providential mercy. The statement is psychologically complex: does forgetting his father's household mean healing, or is it a sign of Egypt's assimilation erasing his origins?
52: אֶפְרַיִם כִּי-הִפְרַנִי אֱלֹהִים בְּאֶרֶץ עָנְיִי (ʾeprayim kî-hiprānî ʾĔlōhîm bĕʾereṣ ʿonyî) — "Ephraim, for God has made me fruitful in the land of my affliction." ʾEprayim from hiprānî (Hiphil of pārāh, to be fruitful, to cause fruitfulness; the Hiphil causative: God made fruitful; cf. Gen 1:22, 28: pĕrû ûrĕḇû, "be fruitful and multiply" — the creation blessing now applied to Joseph's Egyptian sojourn). ʿOnî (affliction, poverty — the same word used for Israel's bondage in Egypt in Exod 3:7 ["I have seen the affliction of my people"]; and for Leah's affliction seen by YHWH in Gen 29:32). Joseph names his Egypt experience "the land of my affliction" — Egypt is simultaneously the place of his fruitfulness and his suffering.
53: The transition verse: "the seven years of abundance that existed in Egypt came to an end." Tāmmannāh (they were completed — Qal of tāmam, to be complete, finished; the years ran their full course exactly as predicted). The transition is stark and brief — one verse for seven years of abundance ending; the brevity mirrors the famine's erasure of the abundance.
54: וַתָּחֵל שֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי הָרָעָב לָבוֹא כַּאֲשֶׁר אָמַר יוֹסֵף — "the seven years of famine began, just as Joseph said." Kāʾăšer ʾāmar yôsēp (just as Joseph said) — the fulfillment-formula; the narrative credits Joseph's interpretation as having been accurate; the proof of prophetic interpretation is fulfillment (cf. Deut 18:22). Rāʿāḇ bĕḵol-hāʾărāṣôt (famine in all the lands) — the scope expands beyond Egypt: the famine is international, not local; this sets up the brothers' journey from Canaan.
55: וַתִּרְעַב כָּל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם (wattiṛʿaḇ kol-ʾereṣ miṣrāyim) — "all the land of Egypt was hungry." Rāʿēḇ (to be hungry — the verb matching the noun rāʿāḇ, famine/hunger); Egypt itself, despite the stored grain, is hungry. לְכוּ אֶל-יוֹסֵף אֲשֶׁר-יֹאמַר לָכֶם תַּעֲשׂוּ (go to Joseph; what he says to you, do it) — Pharaoh's directive echoes the Egyptian idiom of total delegation; and it strikingly echoes the words that Mary will say at Cana: "whatever he says to you, do it" (John 2:5; drawing on the same structural pattern of absolute authority). Joseph is the one to whom all Egypt is directed.
56: וַיִּפְתַּח יוֹסֵף אֶת-כָּל-אֲשֶׁר בָּהֶם (wayyipttaḥ yôsēp ʾet-kol-ʾăšer bāhem) — "Joseph opened all that was in them." The verb pātaḥ (to open — the opening of the storehouses; the same word used for God "opening" the womb [29:31; 30:22] and "opening" the heavens [Gen 7:11]). Joseph "sells" (wayyišbōr, from šābar, to buy/sell grain — a grain-trade verb; Amos 8:5-6 uses the same root for the dishonest grain-sellers; the root is specifically for grain-commerce).
57: וְכָל-הָאָרֶץ בָּאוּ מִצְרַיְמָה לִשְׁבֹּר אֶל-יוֹסֵף (wĕḵol-hāʾāreṣ bāʾû miṣrāymāh lišbōr ʾel-yôsēp) — "all the earth came to Egypt to buy grain from Joseph." Kol-hāʾāreṣ (all the earth/land) — the scope is universal; Joseph has become the grain-provider for the known world. The phrase that will bring his brothers: the famine that was universal means Canaan too is affected; Jacob's sons will soon be among those who "came to Egypt to buy grain from Joseph." The chapter ends with Joseph at the center of the world's food supply — the dreamer of sheaves bowed to by his brothers has become the actual supplier of grain to all nations.
